topics.title: Hydration: More Important Than You Think
topics.answer: Water supports nearly every bodily function, including temperature regulation, joint lubrication, digestion, and workout performance. A common baseline is around 30-35ml of water per kilogram of bodyweight per day, with more needed on hot days or during intense training when you sweat heavily.
tips: Thirst is a lagging indicator - if you're thirsty, you're often already a bit behind.
Urine that's pale yellow is generally a good sign of adequate hydration.
Increase intake on hot days or during longer, sweatier workouts.
dos: Sip water steadily throughout the day rather than chugging large amounts at once.
Carry a reusable water bottle to make drinking water more convenient.
Rehydrate with electrolytes after very heavy sweating (long or intense sessions, hot weather).
donts: Don't rely only on feeling thirsty to remind you to drink.
Don't replace most of your water intake with sugary drinks or excessive caffeine.
Don't overdo it to the extreme either - balance matters, especially during endurance events.
topics.example: A 75kg person might aim for roughly 2.6-3L of water per day as a general baseline, more on training days.

topics.title: How to Increase Your Daily Water Intake
topics.answer: If you struggle to hit your hydration goals, small habit changes usually work better than trying to force it all at once. Building water into existing routines - like drinking a glass with each meal - makes the habit stick more naturally.
tips: Keep a marked water bottle nearby as a visual reminder.
Drink a glass of water first thing in the morning and with each meal.
Flavor water naturally with lemon, cucumber, or berries if plain water feels boring.
dos: Set simple triggers - a glass before each meal, or after each bathroom break.
Track intake for a few days if you're unsure how much you're really drinking.
Adjust intake up on workout days and in hot weather.
donts: Don't try to chug your entire daily goal in one sitting.
Don't rely purely on willpower - build simple habits and reminders instead.
Don't ignore signs of dehydration like dark urine, headache, or fatigue.
topics.example: Drinking a full glass of water with breakfast, lunch, and dinner alone can add close to a liter to your daily total.
